title: Monty Hall Problem

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-04-probability-theory.md

setup.narrative: Three doors. One hides a car; the other two hide goats. You pick a door (call it door A). The host Monty -- who KNOWS where the car is -- opens one of the OTHER two doors, always revealing a goat. You're then offered the choice to either KEEP your original pick or SWITCH to the remaining unopened door.
rules: Monty's reveal is always a goat (he never reveals the car).
Monty NEVER opens your originally-picked door.
If your initial pick has the car, Monty has TWO goats to reveal (he picks one).
If your initial pick has a goat, Monty MUST reveal the unique goat behind the OTHER door he could open.
setup.question: Should you switch? What's P(win) for each strategy?

learningObjectives: Recognize that P(initial pick has car) = 1/3 -- this doesn't change after Monty's reveal.
Compute P(switch wins) = P(initial pick was wrong) = 2/3.
Understand WHY Monty's reveal is informative (he KNOWS where the car is).

stages.label: P(initial pick is the car)
stages.prompt: Before Monty does anything, what is the probability your randomly-picked door has the car?
stages.explanation: Three equally likely doors, one car. P(your door has car) = 1/3.

stages.label: Why doesn't Monty's reveal change P(your door has car)?
stages.prompt: Monty opens a goat-door. Why does P(your door has car) STAY at 1/3?

options.text: Because Monty's reveal gives no NEW information about your door specifically -- he was always going to reveal a goat. The conditional probability P(your door has car | Monty reveals a goat behind another door) = P(your door has car) = 1/3.
It changes to 1/2 because two doors remain.

stages.explanation: Monty would have revealed a goat regardless of where the car is. So 'Monty reveals a goat' is always true -- not new evidence. Your door's probability stays 1/3.

stages.label: Why is the OTHER door now 2/3?
stages.prompt: If P(your door has car) is still 1/3 after Monty's reveal, what's P(the OTHER unopened door has car)?

options.text: 2/3.  Total probability sums to 1, and Monty's opened door has 0 (it's a goat). So 1/3 + 0 + (other) = 1, giving 'other' = 2/3.

stages.explanation: Probability redistributes: the 1/3 that 'belonged to' the opened door (now known to have a goat) shifts entirely to the OTHER unopened door. Result: 1/3 (your door) + 0 (opened) + 2/3 (other) = 1.

stages.label: Commit: should you switch?

options.text: SWITCH.  P(win | switch) = 2/3.  P(win | stay) = 1/3.  Switching DOUBLES your odds.
Stay.  Sticking with your gut wins more often.
Doesn't matter -- both are 50/50 after the reveal.

stages.explanation: SWITCH. Equivalent rephrasing: 'switching wins iff your original pick was wrong' -- which happens with probability 2/3. The intuition trap is treating the post-reveal situation as a fresh 50/50 between two doors, ignoring that Monty's choice was constrained.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: What if Monty DIDN'T know where the car was and revealed randomly (sometimes showing the car)? What's P(switch wins | Monty reveals a goat)?
Generalize to N doors: pick one, Monty opens N-2 goats, you switch. P(win)?
Why is the human intuition for 50/50 so persistent? What's the cognitive bug?
